body.p:
It does not always make sense to search through a tree structure. Although this is blazingly fast in an XML database because every node of the tree is initially included in an index. There are still use cases where you would prefer a relational view of the data.
UWE test footnote (34/40, callout H): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
In MarkLogic, the solution for this is called SQL Views.
For example, you need a relational view of the data when a report needs to be generated across different data sets.
Let's assume the following documents exist in the database:
If you want to display all customers who have placed an order — that would be Alex and Horst — you would use a JOIN in a relational approach, like this:
body.pre: SELECT name, datum, preis FROM kunden k INNER JOIN bestellungen b ON k.id = b.kunde_id
body.p: In a relational view, this would give us the following table:

To define an SQL View for MarkLogic, you use a mechanism called Template Driven Extraction Template Driven Extraction wird verwendet um in MarkLogic eine relationale Sicht auf die baumstrukturierten Daten zu setzen
For this, templates are declared in XML and inserted into the template collection. For our example above, we would need two templates that look like this:
Embedded in XQuery, you could then query the defined SQL Views with the following command:
body.pre: xdmp:sql("SELECT name, datum, preis FROM kunden_view k INNER JOIN bestellungen_view b ON k.id = b.kunde_id")
body.p: The following shows the complete example for a MarkLogic XQuery console session ...
... with a nice tabular output in the lower panel of the Query Console — or as JSON:
